title: STEMI and NSTEMI Pathophysiology and Management

content.definition: Acute coronary syndrome is acute myocardial ischaemia from reduced coronary oxygen supply. It includes unstable angina, NSTEMI and STEMI, and in CTICU it may present as pain, dyspnoea, new ST change, arrhythmia, low cardiac output, rising lactate or unexplained haemodynamic collapse.
STEMI is acute myocardial infarction with persistent ST elevation or a STEMI-equivalent pattern, usually reflecting acute coronary occlusion and transmural injury. It is a reperfusion emergency.
NSTEMI is myocardial infarction with rise or fall in troponin above the assay 99th percentile in a clinical setting of ischaemia, but without persistent diagnostic ST elevation.

content.text: In cardiothoracic ICU, ACS must be interpreted through the operation. After CABG, think graft occlusion, graft spasm, distal target failure, incomplete revascularisation, anaemia, hypotension and demand ischaemia. After valve, TAVI or aortic surgery, think coronary ostial compromise, embolism, air, conduction injury, prosthetic dysfunction, myocardial stunning and low cardiac output syndrome.

items: Acquire a clean 12-lead ECG immediately and repeat it for pain, dyspnoea, hypotension, arrhythmia, rising lactate or vasoactive escalation.
Add posterior leads V7 to V9 for suspicious V1 to V3 ST depression, and right-sided leads V3R to V4R for inferior MI with hypotension, raised CVP or clear lungs.
Assess arterial waveform, MAP, pulse pressure, cardiac index if available, urine output, lactate, ABG, electrolytes, Hb, troponin trend and vasoactive dose trend.
Correct oxygen supply-demand threats: hypoxaemia, anaemia, hypotension, tachyarrhythmia, bradyarrhythmia, fever, shivering, pain, agitation and high work of breathing.
Escalate STEMI, STEMI-equivalent, refractory ischaemia, shock, malignant arrhythmia, complete heart block or suspected graft/coronary complication immediately.

content.text: Do not wait for troponin if the ECG and physiology suggest STEMI or STEMI-equivalent. In a sedated post-cardiac surgery patient, ECG change, ventricular ectopy, rising lactate or low output may be the only chest pain equivalent.
Acute coronary syndrome overview

ACS is a spectrum of acute myocardial ischaemia. The myocardium normally extracts a high proportion of delivered oxygen, so it has limited extraction reserve. When coronary flow falls or myocardial demand rises, ischaemia first impairs relaxation, then systolic contraction, then electrical stability. This is why ACS can present as pulmonary oedema, hypotension, AV block, VT, VF, rising lactate or cardiac arrest before a classic chest pain story is obtained.
Coronary plaque rupture and thrombosis

Type 1 MI usually begins with plaque rupture or erosion. Platelets adhere and activate, thrombin generates fibrin, and a thrombus partially or completely occludes the coronary lumen. Distal embolisation can obstruct microcirculation even after the epicardial vessel is opened. Full occlusion more often produces STEMI; subtotal, transient or collateralised occlusion may produce NSTEMI.
Supply-demand mismatch

Type 2 MI occurs when myocardial oxygen demand exceeds supply without plaque rupture as the primary event. CTICU examples include Hb 60-80 g/L with bleeding, vasoplegia with MAP below target, rapid AF at 150/min, sustained bradycardia at 35/min, hypoxaemia, hypercarbia, acidosis, sepsis, fever, shivering, severe hypertension, high catecholamine doses and low cardiac output syndrome. Correct the cause while ensuring acute coronary occlusion is not missed.
STEMI pathophysiology

STEMI usually reflects acute epicardial coronary occlusion. The injured myocardium develops voltage gradients between ischaemic and non-ischaemic tissue, producing ST elevation in leads facing the injured wall. Necrosis begins subendocardially and progresses outward if reperfusion does not occur. Primary PCI is preferred when timely, generally when reperfusion can occur within 120 minutes from STEMI diagnosis.
NSTEMI pathophysiology

NSTEMI usually reflects subendocardial infarction from partial occlusion, transient occlusion, distal embolisation or severe supply-demand stress. The ECG may show ST depression, T-wave inversion, transient ST elevation, pseudo-normalisation or no diagnostic abnormality. A normal initial ECG does not exclude NSTEMI, particularly early or with posterior/lateral ischaemia.
ECG changes in STEMI and NSTEMI

rows: ST elevation in contiguous leads
Transmural injury or occlusion MI until proven otherwise.
Activate reperfusion/cardiology/surgical pathway.
ST depression V1-V3
Posterior MI mirror pattern if horizontal with ischaemic context.
Record V7-V9 and escalate.
Inferior ST elevation with III greater than II
RCA territory and possible RV infarction.
Record V3R/V4R; avoid preload reduction.
Diffuse ST depression with aVR elevation
Left main, proximal LAD or severe multivessel ischaemia pattern.
Treat as high-risk ACS with shock potential.
Dynamic ST depression or T-wave inversion
NSTE-ACS or demand ischaemia depending on context.
Repeat ECGs and correlate with troponin/haemodynamics.

content.text: Common STEMI thresholds are ST elevation in V2-V3 of at least 2.5 mm in men under 40, 2.0 mm in men 40 or older, 1.5 mm in women, or at least 1.0 mm in other contiguous leads. These thresholds are guides, not substitutes for clinical judgement in paced rhythm, LBBB, LVH, posterior MI or post-operative patients.
Reciprocal ECG changes

Reciprocal ST depression is ST depression in leads electrically opposite the infarct. Inferior STEMI often depresses I and aVL. Posterior MI appears as anterior ST depression in V1 to V3. Reciprocal change strengthens the diagnosis of acute occlusion and helps distinguish true infarction from benign ST elevation.
Posterior MI recognition

Posterior MI is suspected with horizontal ST depression in V1 to V3, tall R waves, upright T waves and an ischaemic clinical picture. Place V7 at the posterior axillary line, V8 below the scapular tip and V9 paraspinal, all level with V6. ST elevation of 0.5 mm or more in V7-V9 is significant in context; 1.0 mm or more is more specific.
Right ventricular MI recognition

RV infarction usually follows proximal RCA occlusion. The pattern is inferior MI with hypotension, raised CVP/JVP, clear lungs and marked preload sensitivity. Confirm with V3R/V4R, especially V4R. Avoid nitrates, aggressive diuresis and excessive PEEP until RV preload and function are assessed.
Troponin interpretation and kinetics

Troponin identifies myocardial injury, not the mechanism. Acute MI requires a rise or fall above the 99th percentile plus evidence of ischaemia. After cardiac surgery, troponin is commonly elevated because of surgical manipulation, cardioplegia, cross-clamp and reperfusion. The concerning pattern is dynamic rise with ECG change, new low output, ventricular arrhythmia or new regional wall-motion abnormality.
Immediate management priorities

items: Call senior help early for STEMI, high-risk NSTEMI, shock, malignant arrhythmia or post-operative graft/coronary complication.
Use oxygen for hypoxaemia, shock, pulmonary oedema or high work of breathing; a common target is SpO2 92-96 percent unless a different target is prescribed.
Avoid nitrates in hypotension, suspected RV infarction, severe aortic stenosis, marked preload dependence or recent phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitor use.
Prepare antiplatelet and anticoagulant therapy after checking operation type, chest drain output, coagulation, Hb trend, renal function and planned procedural strategy.
Prepare for PCI, theatre, echo or mechanical support if the patient has occlusion MI, cardiogenic shock or suspected graft/coronary complication.

content.text: Post-PCI nursing priorities

items: Receive handover covering culprit vessel, stent type, residual disease, access site, anticoagulant dose and antiplatelet plan.
Assess radial or femoral access for bleeding, haematoma, limb ischaemia, pseudoaneurysm or retroperitoneal bleeding signs.
Trend ECG, pain, rhythm, MAP, pulse pressure, lactate, urine output and oxygenation because reperfusion does not guarantee stable myocardium.
Watch for reperfusion arrhythmias including accelerated idioventricular rhythm, VT, VF, bradycardia and AV block.
Escalate recurrent pain, recurrent ST elevation, new ST depression, shock or pulmonary oedema as possible acute closure, stent thrombosis, no-reflow, bleeding or mechanical complication.

content.text: Haemodynamic consequences of MI

LV infarction reduces contractility, stroke volume and cardiac output, raises LV end-diastolic pressure and can cause pulmonary oedema. RV infarction raises right-sided pressures but reduces pulmonary flow and LV preload. Ischaemia destabilises conduction and repolarisation, producing AF, VT, VF, sinus node dysfunction and AV block.
Posterior MI is easy to miss because the standard 12-lead sees it as a mirror image. Suspicious V1 to V3 ST depression needs posterior leads.
Inferior MI with hypotension and clear lungs is RV infarction until proven otherwise. GTN and aggressive diuresis can cause abrupt collapse.
After cardiac surgery, troponin is expected to be elevated. Dynamic rise plus ECG, echo or haemodynamic deterioration is the dangerous pattern.
A sedated patient cannot report chest pain. Rising lactate, new ectopy or low output may be the pain equivalent.
Do not label post-operative ST change as demand ischaemia until graft/coronary occlusion, valve complication and tamponade have been considered.
ECG monitor and 12-lead acquisition

Continuous ECG monitoring detects rhythm instability, but a diagnostic 12-lead localises ischaemia. Use correct V1 and V2 placement in the fourth intercostal spaces, avoid high placement, and label posterior or right-sided leads clearly.
Defibrillator and pacing relevance

ACS can deteriorate into VT, VF or high-grade block. Apply pads early for unstable ACS, recurrent VT, frequent ventricular ectopy, complete heart block or severe bradycardia. If epicardial wires are present, check mode, output, sensitivity, battery, connection and mechanical capture.
Haemodynamic monitor correlation

The arterial line shows whether electrical activity produces flow. A pressor-supported MAP without lactate clearance, urine output or pulse pressure is not reassuring. If PA catheter monitoring is present, correlate ECG changes with CI, CVP, PAOP, SVR, PVR and SvO2.

rows: ECG monitor
Lead quality, ST trend, arrhythmia alarms and paced-beat detection.
Prevents missed arrhythmia and false ST alarms.
12-lead ECG
Correct placement plus posterior/right-sided leads when indicated.
Identifies territory and STEMI equivalents.
Defibrillator
Pad contact, sync function and energy selection.
VT/VF or unstable tachyarrhythmia requires immediate shock capability.
Pacing box
Mode, rate, output, sensitivity, threshold and wire connection.
Inferior MI and valve surgery can cause bradycardia or heart block.
Arterial line
Level, zero, damping, pulse pressure and waveform morphology.
Shows mechanical consequence of ischaemia and rhythm.
12-lead ECG
Immediately, ideally within 10 minutes
Repeat with pain, instability, arrhythmia or haemodynamic change.
V2-V3 STEMI threshold
Men under 40 at least 2.5 mm; men 40 or older at least 2.0 mm; women at least 1.5 mm
Use with contiguous leads and context.
Other lead STEMI threshold
At least 1.0 mm in two contiguous leads
Consider LVH, LBBB, pacing and prior ECG.
Posterior STEMI threshold
At least 0.5 mm in V7-V9; 1.0 mm is more specific
Record posterior leads when V1-V3 show suspicious depression.
Primary PCI
Preferred if reperfusion can occur within 120 minutes from diagnosis
Do not wait for biomarkers if STEMI is diagnostic.
SpO2
Usually 92-96 percent
Treat hypoxaemia, shock and pulmonary oedema; avoid unnecessary hyperoxia.
MAP
At least 65 mmHg; often 70-80 mmHg after cardiac surgery depending on target
Interpret with lactate, urine output and cardiac output.
Cardiac index
Aim above 2.2 L/min/m2
Below 2.0-2.2 with hypoperfusion suggests low-output state.
Potassium
Reduces AF, VT and VF risk.
Magnesium
At least 1.0 mmol/L
Reduces atrial and ventricular irritability.

content.text: Escalate new chest pain, dynamic ST/T change, new arrhythmia, rising lactate, falling urine output or increasing vasoactive support.
Escalate urgently for STEMI, posterior/RV MI pattern, high-risk NSTEMI, post-CABG ischaemia, new low cardiac output or recurrent ischaemia after PCI.
Activate emergency response for VF, pulseless VT, unstable VT, cardiogenic shock, complete heart block with shock, suspected rupture, tamponade, acute MR or VSD.

quiz.question: A post-CABG patient develops new ST elevation, rising lactate and escalating noradrenaline. What is the safest interpretation?
options: Possible graft or coronary complication requiring immediate escalation.
Artefact unless the patient reports pain.
quiz.explanation: New ST elevation with low-output physiology after CABG may indicate graft occlusion, spasm, thrombosis or peri-operative MI.

quiz.question: Which ECG pattern should trigger posterior leads?
options: Horizontal ST depression in V1-V3 with ischaemic context.
quiz.explanation: Posterior MI often appears as reciprocal anterior ST depression in V1-V3.

quiz.question: Why can GTN be dangerous in inferior STEMI with hypotension and clear lungs?
options: It may collapse preload in RV infarction.
quiz.explanation: RV infarction is preload dependent; nitrates reduce venous return and can collapse MAP.

quiz.question: What makes post-operative troponin concerning?
options: Dynamic rise with new ECG change, low output, arrhythmia or new regional wall-motion abnormality.
One unchanged elevated value after bypass.
quiz.explanation: Troponin commonly rises after surgery; the dangerous pattern is dynamic plus clinical, ECG, echo or haemodynamic deterioration.
